sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Tyler, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing.
He reports that it feels like something is stuck in his throat, which is painful and interferes with swallowing solid foods, but not liquids.
He denies coughing, vomiting blood, or weight loss.
Past medical history: Diabetes and high blood pressure.
He is currently taking metformin 500mg twice daily and norvasc.
He has been compliant with his medications and has been checking his blood sugar levels 80% of the time, with numbers within target range.
Review of systems: Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or any other side effects from medications.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice daily
Norvasc (dosage and frequency not specified)
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling, and his thyroid feels normal.
No masses or lymph nodes are palpable.
The throat examination is normal, with no masses or swelling.
The lung and heart exams are clear, with no murmurs.
The belly exam reveals epigastric tenderness, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back and extremity exams are normal, and the neurological exam is fine.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with difficulty swallowing, likely due to a possible narrowing of the esophagus, possibly a stricture.
Problem list: Dysphagia; Esophageal stricture suspected; Diabetes; Hypertension.
Plan:
I recommended a referral to gastroenterology for a scope to evaluate the esophagus.
I also prescribed Prilosec 20mg once daily for possible reflux.
I advised the patient to chew foods slowly and carefully, especially solid foods.
I will order another hemoglobin A1c and kidney function tests.
I will also give the patient an eye referral to ophthalmology to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I will continue the patient on metformin and norvasc, and schedule a follow-up appointment in a month to review the results of the blood tests.
Follow-up: The patient will return in a month for follow-up, or sooner if symptoms worsen or if there are any concerns.
